answers.intake_q1: When did your shoulder pain start?
answers.intake_q2: Can you describe where exactly in your shoulder the pain is located?
answers.intake_q3: Any recent injury or trauma to your shoulder?
answers.intake_a3: I think I injured it playing basketball
answers.intake_q4: Can you tell me if you have any weakness or difficulty moving your shoulder?
answers.intake_a4: no - it just hurts when I lift it
answers.intake_q5: Any fever, chills, or redness around the shoulder?
answers.intake_q6: Have you tried any treatments or medications for your shoulder pain, and did they help?
answers.intake_a6: icing but only the first day and it helped, but not much
answers.intake_q7: Any numbness, tingling, or pain radiating down your arm?
answers.intake_q8: Any prior history of shoulder problems or arthritis?
answers.intake_q9: Any difficulty or pain when you try to raise your arm above your head or behind your back?
answers.intake_a9: raising my arm above my head hurts a lot, but behin my back is fine
answers.intake_q10: Any swelling or bruising around your shoulder?

gating_notes: liver disease status is yes/unknown (avoid hepatotoxic meds / adjust dosing)

note.soap:
S: 
The patient is a 34-year-old female presenting with a 2-week history of pain localized to the side of her shoulder. The pain began after a suspected injury while playing basketball. She reports no weakness or difficulty moving the shoulder except pain when lifting the arm above the head. There is no associated fever, chills, redness, swelling, bruising, numbness, tingling, or radiation of pain down the arm. She has no prior history of shoulder problems or arthritis. Initial treatment with icing provided minimal relief after the first day. Patient is not pregnant.

O: 
Phone evaluation only; no physical exam or vital signs available.

A: 
The working diagnosis is shoulder impingement syndrome likely related to recent trauma and activity. This is supported by localized lateral shoulder pain exacerbated by arm elevation without systemic symptoms or neurological deficits.

Differential: 
1. Shoulder impingement syndrome 
2. Rotator cuff strain or tear 
3. Frozen shoulder (adhesive capsulitis) 
4. Shoulder contusion or minor soft tissue injury 

P: 
- First-line medication: Acetaminophen for pain relief, dosing per label instructions, duration as needed, no contraindications noted (EVID_4). 
- Alternatives: 
 - Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) such as ibuprofen, if no liver disease contraindications, dosing per label, duration as needed. 
 - Physical therapy referral for guided shoulder exercises if pain persists or functional limitation develops. 
- Medication safety considerations: Avoid NSAIDs due to underlying liver disease; acetaminophen should be used cautiously and not exceed recommended doses. No pregnancy or renal disease concerns. 
- Return precautions: Seek in-person evaluation if pain worsens, weakness develops, fever or redness occurs, or if there is no improvement within 48–72 hours. 
- Testing: If no improvement within 48–72 hours, worsening symptoms, or recurrent symptoms: seek in-person evaluation for appropriate testing (e.g., urinalysis/urine culture or other workup as indicated).

evidence.title: Shoulder pain (starter evidence summary)
evidence.snippet: Most shoulder pain is overuse/tendon-related. Trauma, inability to move the arm, or cardiac symptoms require urgent evaluation.

evidence.title: Frozen shoulder - NHS
evidence.snippet: et a frozen shoulder. Frozen shoulder happens when the tissue around your shoulder joint becomes inflamed. The tissue then gets tighter and shrinks, which causes pain.
